elements.sent-2411-001: This paper presents an investigation into the high-order asymptotic expansion for 2D and 3D cubic nonlinear Klein-Gordon equations in the non-relativistic limit regime.
elements.sent-2411-002: There are extensive numerical and analytic results concerning that the solution of NLKG can be approximated by first-order modulated Schr\"odinger profiles in terms of $\ei{ v + c.c.}
elements.sent-2411-003: Particularly, the best analytic result up to now is given in \cite{lei , which proves that the $L_x^2$ norm of the error can be controlled by $\ve^2 +(\ve^2t)^{\frac \al 4}$ for $H^\al_x$-data, $\al \in [1, 4]$.}
elements.sent-2411-004: As for the high-order expansion, to our best knowledge, there are only numerical results, while the theoretical one is lacking.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we extend this study further and give the first high-order analytic result.
elements.sent-2411-006: We introduce the high-order expansion inspired by the numerical experiments in \cite{schratz2020, faou2014a : \begin{equation}\ei{}v +\ve^2 \Big( \frac 18 \ei{3} v^3 +\ei{} w \Big) +c.c.,\end{equation} where $w$ is the solution to some specific Schr\"odinger-type equation.}
elements.sent-2411-007: We show that the $L_x^2$ estimate of the error is of higher order $\ve^4+\left(\ve^2t\right)^\frac{\al {4}$ for $H^\al_x$-data, $\al \in [4, 8]$.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Besides, some counter-examples are given to suggest the sharpness of this upper bound.
elements.sent-2411-009: In this paper, we consider the following cubic nonlinear Klein-Gordon(NLKG) equation in the non-relativistic limit regime for dimension $d=2, 3$, \eq{ \ve^2 \p_{tt u^{\ve -\De u^{\ve + \frac{1 {\ve^2 u^{\ve = -|u^{\ve |^2u^{\ve , u^{\ve (0)=u_0, \quad \p_t u^{\ve (0)=\frac{u_1 {\ve^2 , where the solution $u^\ve (t,x): \R\times\R^d \to \R$ and initial data $u_0, u_1$ are real-valued and independent of $\ve$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Here $\ve$ is inversely proportional to the speed of light and the non-relativistic limit regime means the regime when the speed of light goes to infinity or equivalently $\ve $ approaches 0.
elements.sent-2411-011: The NLKG equation in the non-relativistic limit serves as a mathematical model for describing a free particle with spin zero.
elements.sent-2411-012: This equation also can be regarded as the relativistic counterpart of the Schr\"odinger equation, specifically designed to characterize the kinetic energy component of scalar particles.
elements.sent-2411-013: The equation \eqref{eq:u conserves the energy \begin{equation}E(t) := \int_{\R^d} \left(\ve^{2} \left|\p_{t}u^{\ve}\right|^2 +\left|\na u^{\ve}\right|^2 +\ve^{-2}\left|u^{\ve}\right|^2 +\frac{1}{2}\left|u^{\ve}\right|^4 \right)dx =\int_{\R^d} \Big[ \ve^{-2} \left( \left| u_0\right|^2 +\left| u_1\right|^2 \right) +\left|\na u_0\right|^2 +\frac{1}{2}\left|u_0\right|^4 \Big]dx =E(0).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The NLKG equation \eqref{eq:u in the non-relativistic regime has been extensively studied in both mathematical and physical fields.}
elements.sent-2411-015: It is well-known that the NLKG equation reduces to the nonlinear Schr\"odinger(NLS) equation in this limit, providing a mathematical tool for bridging the gap between relativistic and non-relativistic regimes.
elements.sent-2411-016: Here, we highlight a crucial observation that the solution exhibits high-frequency oscillations over time within this regime.
elements.sent-2411-017: The presence of oscillations makes classical integration schemes invalid and gives rise to challenges in numerical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-018: Furthermore, complications emerge in analysis wherein solutions may not adhere to the uniform $H^1_x$ bound as the energy lacks uniform boundedness when $\varepsilon $ approaches 0.
elements.subsection-2411-001: High-order modulated expansion to Schr\"odinger profiles
elements.sent-2411-019: Firstly, we introduce the modulated Fourier expansion also as a WKB expansion to approximate the solution $u^\varepsilon$ in terms of the modulated profiles.
elements.sent-2411-020: The modulated Fourier expansion has been extensively utilized in the numerical analysis of oscillatory problems \cite{cohen2003, faou2013, hairer2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The basic idea is to expand solution $u^\ve$ as follows \begin{equation}u^\ve(t)=\sum_{m\in \N_+}e^{i\frac{mt}{\ve^2}} u_m (t),\end{equation} where all time derivatives of profiles $u _m(t)$ is uniformly bounded as $\ve $ approaches to $ 0$.
elements.sent-2411-022: In particular, these modulated profiles are typically selected as solutions to the cubic NLS and thus called Schr\"odinger profiles.
elements.sent-2411-023: We note that those profiles $u_m(x)$ are also allowed to be $\ve $-dependent oscillating and the corresponding expansion is called multiscale frequency expansion.
elements.sent-2411-024: We will see the expansion involving Schr\"odinger-wave profiles below is an example of this type of expansion.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, we focus on the modulated Fourier expansion involving Schr\"odinger profiles in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we introduce a high-order modulated Fourier expansion inspired by the numerical study in \cite{schratz2020, faou2014a :.}
